channel.title: NYT > Technology
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: OpenAI’s Fog of War + Betting on Iran + Hard Fork Review of Slop
item.description: “The Pentagon and OpenAI are saying to the public, You’re just going to have to trust us. And the public is saying, Well, we don’t.”
item.dc:creator: Kevin Roose, Casey Newton, Rachel Cohn, Whitney Jones, Vjeran Pavic, Chris Wood, Dan Powell, Elisheba Ittoop and Rowan Niemisto
item.media:credit: Photo Illustration by The New York Times; Photo: Mandel Ngan/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images

item.title: Pentagon Officially Notifies Anthropic It Is a ‘Supply Chain Risk’
item.description: Anthropic has said it will sue the Defense Department over the designation, which could prevent the start-up from doing business with the U.S. government.
item.dc:creator: Cade Metz, Julian E. Barnes and Sheera Frenkel
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Although Anthropic is the only company that provides the Pentagon with artificial intelligence technologies for use on classified systems, other companies are angling to replace it.

item.title: S.E.C. Settles Its Case Against Justin Sun, an Investor in the Trumps’ Crypto Coins
item.description: The decision to settle the court action is particularly striking because the defendant was accused of a serious violation of securities laws. He denied the charges.
item.dc:creator: Sharon LaFraniere and David Yaffe-Bellany
item.media:credit: Jason Andrew for The New York Times
item.media:description: Justin Sun, founder of the crypto platform TRON, on his way to the White House for a private tour last year. Mr. Sun threw Mr. Trump’s fledgling crypto company a cash lifeline at a time when it was struggling.

item.title: Demand for AI Data Centers Sends Prospectors Hunting for Land and Power
item.description: Brian Janous, a former Microsoft executive, and his firm Cloverleaf have become modern-day land men, packaging electricity and land for data centers.
item.dc:creator: Tripp Mickle and Tim Gruber
item.category: Vantage Data Centers Management Co LLC
item.media:credit: Tim Gruber for The New York Times
item.media:description: Brian Janous, co-founder of Cloverleaf Infrastructure. His start-up has been thrust into heated community debates over data center development.

item.title: For OpenAI and Anthropic, the Competition Is Deeply Personal
item.description: A fight over Pentagon contracts shows how the leaders of Silicon Valley’s two most important A.I. start-ups are feuding over the future of the tech industry.
item.dc:creator: Mike Isaac

item.title: Anthropic’s and OpenAI’s Dance With the Pentagon: What to Know
item.description: Negotiations, threats and amended contracts have left plenty of questions. Here are some answers.
item.dc:creator: Cade Metz
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth declared Anthropic a “supply chain risk,” a rare designation for an American company.

item.title: ‘Designed to Wreak Havoc’: The Cheap Drones Shaping the War With Iran
item.description: Iran has launched waves of Shahed drones to menace Persian Gulf nations. The U.S. has unleashed its own copycat on Iran. It’s a sign of how war is changing.
item.dc:creator: Paul Mozur and Adam Satariano
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Are A.I.-Generated Videos Changing How We See Animals?
item.description: By manipulating animals to do wonderful things, we may become numb to their real wonder.
item.dc:creator: Sophie Haigney

item.title: From 2017: Frances Gabe, Creator of the Only Self-Cleaning Home, Dies at 101
item.description: Equal parts quixotic dreamer and accomplished visionary, Ms. Gabe made the house do its own scrubbing.
item.dc:creator: Margalit Fox
item.media:description: Frances Gabe put the kitchen of her self-cleaning house through its paces in 2002.

item.title: The Smart Home Never Quite Worked. Now It’s Getting an A.I. Reboot.
item.description: Amazon and Google think that artificially intelligent assistants like Alexa+ and Gemini will speed up the process of setting up a smart home, but many problems remain unsolved.
item.dc:creator: Brian X. Chen

item.title: How $800 Monthly Car Payments Are Hurting Car Sales
item.description: Cars have become so expensive that many Americans are putting off or not buying new cars, hurting the auto industry.
item.dc:creator: Neal E. Boudette
item.media:description: The average monthly payment for a new car was $774 at the end of last year, and the average new car now costs nearly $50,000.

item.title: You’ve Never Seen Ants Like This Before
item.description: The Antscan database captures the tiny insects’ diversity and shows how the world’s living things could be rendered in three dimensions.
item.dc:creator: Ari Daniel

item.title: A Word to the Wise: Don’t Trust A.I. to File Your Taxes
item.description: The world’s smartest technology is no match for the U.S. tax code.
item.dc:creator: Stuart A. Thompson

item.title: Elon Musk Defends Social Media Posts in Twitter Shareholder Lawsuit
item.description: Mr. Musk said he did not expect his posts in the lead-up to his 2022 acquisition of the social media site, now X, to affect the company’s share price.
item.dc:creator: Kate Conger
item.media:credit: Jason Henry for The New York Times
item.media:description: Elon Musk arriving at the Phillip Burton Federal Building and U.S. Courthouse in San Francisco on Wednesday.

item.title: Trump Announces A.I. Industry Pledge to Pay for Power
item.description: Companies including Google, Microsoft and OpenAI committed to pay for the power plants and grid upgrades needed to run their data centers.
item.dc:creator: David McCabe and Brad Plumer
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump meeting with executives from the artificial intelligence industry in Washington on Wednesday.

item.title: Lawmakers Question Intel’s Use of Tools From Blacklisted Chinese Firm
item.description: A bipartisan group of senators raised national security concerns about the chipmaker, which is now partly owned by taxpayers.
item.dc:creator: Tripp Mickle
item.media:description: People visit the ACM Research booth during a trade fair for the semiconductor industry in Shanghai, in 2023.

item.title: Ford and G.M. Face a Dilemma as China Excels in Electric Vehicles
item.description: General Motors, Ford and other established automakers risk becoming relics if they don’t catch up to Chinese carmakers and technology companies in electric vehicles and self-driving cars.
item.dc:creator: Jack Ewing
item.media:credit: Emily Elconin for The New York Times
item.media:description: A Ford Motor factory in Wayne, Mich. U.S. automakers are facing difficult choices.

item.title: OpenAI Amends A.I. Deal With the Pentagon
item.description: The new pact includes additional protections to prevent the use of the company’s technology for mass surveillance of Americans.
item.dc:creator: Cade Metz and Julian E. Barnes
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: “It’s critical to protect the civil liberties of Americans, and there was so much focus on this, that we wanted to make this point especially clear,” OpenAI’s chief executive, Sam Altman, center, wrote on social media.

item.title: Reporters Seek Comment. What Happens Next May Surprise You.
item.description: Accurate journalism requires full and fair reporting. It means pushing hard to hear from those you’re writing about.
item.dc:creator: Mike Abrams
item.media:credit: Photo Illustration by Mel Haasch; Grace Cary/Getty Images

item.title: The Pentagon’s Favorite Tech Guy Is This Hawaiian Shirt-Wearing Founder
item.description: Palmer Luckey, who founded the defense tech start-up Anduril, has become the It Guy as President Trump aims to modernize the U.S. military with autonomous weapons.
item.dc:creator: Sheera Frenkel and Cade Metz
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces

item.title: Paramount Won Over Warner Bros. Now for the Regulators.
item.description: Global officials are expected to closely examine the $111 billion deal, which ties together two entertainment powerhouses, over potential competition issues.
item.dc:creator: David McCabe
item.media:credit: Mark Abramson for The New York Times
item.media:description: Tourists outside Warner Bros. Studio Tour in Burbank, Calif.

item.title: How Talks Between Anthropic and the Defense Dept. Fell Apart
item.description: The Pentagon and Anthropic were close to agreeing on the use of artificial intelligence. But strong personalities, mutual dislike and a rival company unraveled a deal.
item.dc:creator: Sheera Frenkel, Cade Metz and Julian E. Barnes
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Demetrius Freeman for The New York Times
item.media:description: In January, Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth published a memo calling on A.I. to be widely integrated across the military and for A.I. companies to offer their technology without restrictions.

item.title: Bring On Defunct: The iPod Enthralls Young Music Listeners
item.description: It’s not exactly going analog, but people looking to move away from streaming say Apple’s old device is a small step away from constantly being online.
item.dc:creator: Kalley Huang
